title: Columbia Student RANKS the Ivy Leagues...
body: To the high schoolers and young people that I imagine watch these videos types of videos, please do not put too much weight on a university’s ranking when making your decision. It’s nearly impossible to objectively rank a university and most of, if not all of the ranking methods are flawed in some way. In academia these rankings are NEVER considered and some of the best research is coming out of schools that a lot of students will write off due to their ranking.
author: @michaels8902

title: If Cornell was honest #college #collegeadmissions #ivyleague #cornell
body: I went to Cornell and made a ton of important connections and when I applied to grad school (medical school) a ton of my interviewers were Cornell alum and we were able to connect and just hit off immediately due to the connection.  I eventually went to NYU, where a solid 10% of my classmates were Cornell alum and had a fantastic time.  I’m now starting my academic career in medicine on the west coast, where, again I run into and hit it off with a gaggle of former Cornell grads and have instant connection.  Cornell changed my life, when I can, I will donate back to my Alma mater

title: If Cornell was honest #college #collegeadmissions #ivyleague #cornell
body: I attend Cornell, and there are countless aspects of my school that I genuinely value, including numerous activities like sports, games, club events and so on. I never feel "stuck" inside the walls of Cornell - In fact, I only wish I could have so much time to explore everything that my school has to offer. I never experience any form of inferiority complex because of my association with this institution. You mentioned Andy Bernard and I must confess that I didn't know who he was until you brought him up, so any reference to him is quite irrelevant. It's essential to recognize that allowing one individual to shape your perception of an entire group is a flawed approach. Not everyone gets admitted into a competitive school because of a good SAT score. Instances of legacy admissions are present in various prestigious institutions, including Harvard and Yale, and drawing broad conclusions based on such factors is, in essence, an immature perspective. Let's focus on appreciating the unique qualities of each academic institution without letting isolated incidents overshadow the collective experiences of its diverse student body.
author: @moreblessingmushohwe6506

title: Every IVY league explained in 10 minutes
body:
I graduated from Columbia in '00. Looking back, it seems crazy to me that these schools expect all these things...from 17 year old applicants. Don't worry if you don't have it all figured out already, guys. Just be passionate about learning and studying.

My best advice is to make good friends on campus. Not the "coolest" people you can find. The nicest, with whom you get along the best. Make friendships that will endure and help you through tough times - and be a good friend to others. Be open to friendships with people of different ethnicities, social classes, religious groups, etc. Don't just stay in your comfort zone.

Oh, and don't be a snob, for goodness' sake. You're not better than other people just because you go to an Ivy League school.
author: @kumaranvij

title: Every IVY league explained in 10 minutes
body:
What matters most for success at all the schools of the Ivy League -- and most all schools with highly competitive admissions -- is having a strong ego and self confidence. Public high school students from wealthy suburbs with lots of AP's will have no problem with the academics, But without a strong ego they can become lonely and depressed. Instead of being in the top 5% of their high school class, and knowing that the 95% below are nowhere near as smart, having 100% of one's classmates being every bit as smart -- or more so -- can be intimidating and threatening.

Do NOT attend a highly competitive school to "find yourself". If you've achieved a lot in high school, but you're not so confident, you're better off to attend a smaller school, or less prestigious school, where teachers and coaches will provide you emotional support. Also, you are much more likely to get financial aid. Being a big fish in a small pond is much more fun. In the Ivy League, you're just another fish.
author: @BigGuyBoleslaw

title: Every IVY league explained in 10 minutes
body:
"Fun fact: Columbia has had more Nobel Prize winners affiliated with it than any other US university" - Incorrect. Harvard has had (1) more professors who won the Nobel Prize, (2) more undergraduate alumni who won the Nobel Prize, and (3) more graduate alumni who won the Nobel Prize than any other university in the world. Harvard alumni have also won more Nobel Prizes in each of the following 4 categories than any other university in the world - physics, chemistry, medicine/physiology, and economics.
Columbia does come in second place after Harvard. As of 2023, 29 Nobel Prize-winning undergraduate degree holders from Harvard, 21 from Columbia, 13 from University of Chicago, 12 from MIT, and so forth. 71 Nobel Prize-winning graduate degree holders from Harvard, 39 from Columbia, 32 from University of Chicago, 29 from MIT, 24 from UC Berkeley, and so on.
author: @jy7213

title: RESULTS OF COLLEGES I APPLIED TO AS A CURRENT IVY LEAGUE STUDENT #upenn #ivyleague #college
body: From my personal experience with the 2025 application cycle, after applying to 15 colleges and getting accepted by only 4, I'd say that quality matters more than quantity. I initially wished to get into many 'good' colleges as possible, but if I could go back in time, I would spend more time applying to colleges that truly fit me, regardless of prestige (although colleges do get better as prestige increases). And remember don't just apply to T10 schools, there are other schools that provide awesome college experience and are more affordable that are less heard of.

title: RESULTS OF COLLEGES I APPLIED TO AS A CURRENT IVY LEAGUE STUDENT #upenn #ivyleague #college
body: I'm curious what you picked! I also applied to over 20 schools (Ivys and conservatories). I ended up attending Eastman and University of Rochester. It's the best of both worlds. Amazing education at both schools. As a UR only student you can still take lessons at Eastman or major in music at UR with all the same Eastman faculty. You can also apply to Eastman the following year and get a 5th year of aid. Comp Sci program is fantastic and most of the students I talk to all had incredible high school stats and went to UR after getting rejected from Ivy league. It's a great education here. In any case, best of luck making a decision!

title: If Dartmouth were honest #money #collegestudytips #ivyleague #collegeprep
body:
I live in Hanover! In my opinion, it really is the perfect place to study as there is not much distraction per se. One must really have to enjoy outdoor activities to be happy here though. Look at it this way, you are only as happy as you choose to be. This area can be magical. Specifically if you are from a more progressive area, you should look at your time in the Upper Valley of New Hampshire as a soul-searching journey. Keep your phone at home, venture into the woods and feel the vibration of that which surrounds you…. it’s healing.
On another note, if you ever want to meet up for a drink at Mollys, let me know😂❤

title: Things I Hate and Love About Dartmouth (brutally honest senior) | JustJoelle1
body: Thank you 😊 so much for being honest my daughter a junior at a private prep school and she is Native American we pay tuition like everyone else. And we were told by guidance counselor to look at Dartmouth because of there Native American program. My daughter never experienced racism and I don’t want her in a remote school to were she might very well experience racism and people say she only there because of her being Native American. I being 100 percent Mohawk have always been told by my friends and there parents . That because I’m Native American I get go school for free and get into schools that I would not normally. There is less than 1 percent of native Americans left . It make me sad that no one understands that

title: Things I Hate and Love About Dartmouth (brutally honest senior) | JustJoelle1
body:
Love the video, the one con I was surprised you didn't mention was the lack of resources for those struggling with mental or physical health.

Due to the short length and high stress of each term, becoming seriously mentally or physically ill means that you basically have to drop everything and go home on a medical leave, since the college is super strict about grades and extensions, and because missing as much as a week in some classes can guarantee a C.

And God help you if you become mentally ill because the college counselors are horrible and overworked, and the counselors in the area don't take insurance and charge huge rates.

I met SO many students that had to take a huge amount of time off or just struggle in silence because of a lack of official support and resources. I straight up think it's becoming an epidemic.

But honestly Dartmouth is not different from other colleges in this. I just wish that we'd be better, since we have the funds.
author: @Leroset

title: A Productive Day In My Life at Columbia University
body: Wow, this video was incredibly inspiring! Junzhe, you truly captured what it's like to navigate the hustle and bustle of life at Columbia, and your passion really shines through. Seeing the balance between academics, extracurriculars, and personal time is a reminder of how much effort and determination it takes to pursue your dreams at such a prestigious school. Your perspective not only motivates me to push harder in my own studies, but also to appreciate the little moments that make college life so special. Thank you for sharing this piece of your journey with us, it's a real testament to the power of hard work, resilience, and staying true to yourself. Keep doing amazing things!

title: A Productive Day In My Life at Columbia University
body: funny enough, i didn’t think i needed a book on manifestation until a friend handed me vibrations of manifestation by alex lane. honestly, i thought the whole idea of manifestation was a bit overhyped, but after hearing a bunch of people talk about how it changed their perspective, i thought, “why not?” they weren’t all talking about it in that cheesy way where everything suddenly becomes perfect, but they were definitely more focused, more aware of the energy they put out, and more aligned with what they actually wanted. one of them even started attracting opportunities they’d been chasing for years. i’m not saying it’s the one fix-all answer, but it definitely opened some eyes around me. if you’ve ever felt like you’re not attracting the right things or you’re stuck in a rut, maybe this book could help you shift things.
author: @thatikondasathishkumar9686
